true_relatives.note: Same root as इसका; the near-demonstrative Sanskrit base gives Hindi इस- its 'right here' proximity meaning

useful_coincidences.meaning: third person singular present of 'to be'
useful_coincidences.note: 'is' sounds exactly like the first syllable of इसकी — 'is-key', which sounds like 'is-this-one's-key', a ready-made mnemonic

sound_alikes.note: 'iskī' sounds like 'is-key' — imagine the key belonging to the thing right in front of you to lock in the near-demonstrative possessive meaning

root_breakdown.logic: Identical to इसका except for the feminine suffix -kī; the possessive agrees with the noun owned, not with the referent यह itself.

etymology_story: इसकी is the feminine counterpart of इसका, built from the same oblique base इस- plus the genitive suffix -kī.
इस- comes from Sanskrit idam, one of the proximate demonstrative pronouns meaning 'this thing close by'.
The suffix -kī (feminine) vs. -kā (masculine) follows the standard Hindi possessive agreement pattern.
So इसकी means 'of this one' when the noun it modifies is grammatically feminine — for example, इसकी किताब (this one's book).

tips.tip: the first syllable sounds exactly like the English word 'is'
the ending '-kī' sounds exactly like the English word 'key'

memory_hook.primary: A woman holding up a key labelled 'THIS ONE'S' — it belongs to the small shop right next to her
memory_hook.connection: The key ('-kī' = 'key') right next to the shop (proximity = near demonstrative) with a woman holding it (feminine agreement) captures all three layers of इसकी

examples.english: This one's book is on the table.

examples.hindi: यह बिल्ली है — इसकी आँखें नीली हैं।
examples.romanisation: yah billī hai — iskī ā̃kheñ nīlī haĩ.
examples.english: This is a cat — its eyes are blue.
examples.literal: this cat is — its eyes blue are

examples.english: What is the price of this?

true_relatives.note: उस- is the oblique form of वह (that/he/she), descending from Sanskrit's distal demonstrative root; it refers to something farther away or already mentioned
Both उसका and его are singular third-person possessives covering 'his', 'her', and 'its' depending on context — the same ambiguity exists in both languages

useful_coincidences.note: 'us' sounds like the first syllable of उसका — a false friend but a useful sound hook; just remember उस- points away from the speaker

sound_alikes.note: 'uskā' sounds like 'us-car' — imagine pointing at a car across the street (away from you, hence distant/distal) saying 'that one's car'

evolution.meaning: of that one / his / her / its (masculine)

root_breakdown.logic: The distal root (pointing away) gave Hindi वह (that/he/she) and its oblique stem उस-; adding -kā creates the possessive 'belonging to that one', which serves as 'his', 'her', or 'its' for any singular non-honorific referent.

etymology_story: उसका is formed from उस, the oblique stem of वह (that / he / she), plus the possessive suffix -kā.
वह descends from Sanskrit's distal demonstrative pronouns — words that point to something away from the speaker.
In Hindi, one word covers 'his', 'her', and 'its' for singular non-honorific third persons — context always makes the meaning clear.
The suffix -kā (masculine) changes to -kī (feminine) or -ke (oblique/plural) depending only on the gender of the noun owned.

pronunciation_guide.stress: even; 'us' rhymes with English 'bus', '-kā' is long

tips.tip: the first syllable 'us' sounds like the English word 'bus' without the 'b'
the '-kā' sounds like 'car' without the r

memory_hook.primary: A person on a street corner pointing across the road at a parked car — on the windshield is a sign reading 'HIS / HERS'
memory_hook.connection: Pointing across the street (distance = distal, 'that over there') mirrors उस-'s distal meaning; the 'his/hers' sign captures उसका's gender-neutral third-person possessive role

examples.english: His / her house is very big.

examples.hindi: यह कुत्ता है — उसका खाना कहाँ है?
examples.romanisation: yah kuttā hai — uskā khānā kahā̃ hai?
examples.english: This is a dog — where is its food?
examples.literal: this dog is — its food where is?

true_relatives.note: Sanskrit tasyāḥ was the feminine genitive of the distal demonstrative; Hindi reduced this to the single form उसकी for all genders of referent, changing only the possessed noun's gender
Both उसकी and её can mean 'her' or 'its' for a feminine thing; the possessive agrees with the owned noun in Hindi but with the owner in Russian — a key contrast

sound_alikes.note: 'uskī' is close to 'husky' without the 'h' — picture a husky dog and 'its (feminine) breed' to hook the sound

root_breakdown.logic: Identical to उसका in ancestry; -kī replaces -kā to agree with the feminine noun being possessed — the referent's gender is irrelevant.

etymology_story: उसकी is the feminine form of उसका and shares its full history from Sanskrit's distal demonstrative pronoun tad.
In Hindi, the possessive suffix agrees with the noun owned: -kā for masculine nouns, -kī for feminine nouns.
This means उसकी can mean 'his book', 'her voice', 'its tail' — any singular non-honorific third person, as long as the owned thing is feminine.
Context (or the surrounding sentence) always makes clear whether 'his', 'her', or 'its' is meant.

tips.tip: the first syllable 'us' sounds like 'bus' without the 'b'
the ending '-kī' sounds exactly like the English word 'key'

memory_hook.primary: A person across the road pointing to a key hanging on a hook outside a door — a small sign next to the key reads 'HIS / HERS'
memory_hook.connection: Distance (across the road) signals the distal उस-, the key (-kī) signals the feminine suffix, and 'his/hers' captures the ambiguous third-person meaning

examples.english: His / her book is very good.
His / her mother is in Delhi.

examples.hindi: बिल्ली की आँखें देखो — उसकी आँखें हरी हैं।
examples.romanisation: billī kī ā̃kheñ dekho — uskī ā̃kheñ harī haĩ.
examples.english: Look at the cat's eyes — its eyes are green.
examples.literal: cat's eyes look — its eyes green are

examples.english: His / her voice is very sweet.

true_relatives.note: Sanskrit asmākam is the direct ancestor; the ham- stem comes from a Prakrit reduction of asma- (us)
Both हमारा and наш are the first-person plural possessives descending from PIE *nos- / *nes- (us); they serve the exact same grammatical role
English 'our' and Hindi हमारा share PIE ancestry through the first-person plural pronoun *nos-

useful_coincidences.note: हमारा sounds very close to Arabic ḥimār (donkey) — a memorable false friend; remember: हमारा means 'ours', not a donkey

sound_alikes.note: 'hamārā' starts with 'ham-' like 'hammer' — imagine a group hammering together to build something: 'our project'

root_breakdown.logic: The ancient PIE first-person plural root evolved through Sanskrit's long genitive form (asmākam) into the compact Hindi possessive हमारा; the -rā suffix marks masculine agreement, parallel to मेरा and तेरा.

etymology_story: हमारा descends from the Sanskrit first-person plural pronoun asmad, whose genitive was the long form asmākam (of us).
Through Prakrit this compressed dramatically into the stem hama-, losing the elaborate Sanskrit endings.
Apabhraṁśa added the possessive suffix -āra to produce hamāra, meaning 'belonging to us'.
Modern Hindi uses हमारा as the masculine form of 'our', with हमारी and हमारे for feminine and oblique contexts.

pronunciation_guide.stress: slight stress on the second syllable 'mā'; both long ā's carry the word

tips.tip: the first syllable 'ha' is close to 'hum' — 'ha-mā-rā'
the 'ha-' opening is reminiscent of Russian нам (nam, 'to us') — both point to the first person plural

memory_hook.primary: A group of people surrounding a large table, all placing their hands on it at once — on the table is a sign that reads 'OURS'
memory_hook.connection: Multiple hands on the same table (collective, plural ownership) captures हमारा's first-person plural meaning; the scene anchors 'we/us' as the owners

true_relatives.note: Same root as हमारा; -ī suffix marks feminine noun agreement
Russian наша is exactly the feminine form of наш (our), paralleling how हमारी is the feminine of हमारा — both languages make the possessive agree with the noun owned

sound_alikes.note: 'hamārī' sounds like 'hammer-ee' — the group hammer image from हमारा now ends in the feminine '-ī' suffix, signalling a feminine noun is owned

root_breakdown.logic: Identical ancestry to हमारा; the only change is the final vowel — -ā becomes -ī to agree with a feminine noun, following the same pattern as all Hindi possessives.

etymology_story: हमारी shares every step of its history with हमारा — both derive from the Sanskrit first-person plural genitive asmākam.
The feminine ending -ī was simply substituted for the masculine -ā to make the possessive agree with a feminine noun.
This -ā / -ī / -e pattern runs across all Hindi possessive pronouns: मेरा/मेरी, तेरा/तेरी, हमारा/हमारी.
हमारी is used whenever the thing owned is grammatically feminine: हमारी माँ (our mother), हमारी भाषा (our language).

tips.tip: the first syllable 'ha' is close to 'hum'

memory_hook.primary: A group of women standing in a circle, all holding the same banner that reads 'OURS' — their collective ownership is the scene's centrepiece
memory_hook.connection: The group of women (plural, feminine) holding a shared banner captures both the collective first-person plural 'our' and the feminine agreement of हमारी
